summary: The essay argues that we underestimate how much risk famous scientists took because biographies erase their mistakes and because successful choices now look obvious. Using Newton, Graham notes that in his time physics, alchemy, and theology appeared similarly promising, so his bets were all risky, even though only one paid off.
key_points: Biographies sanitize failures, making historical risk-taking seem smaller than it was.
Success makes past choices look inevitable even when they were uncertain.
Newton pursued multiple lines that looked equally promising in his era.
Risk is inherent in discovery because outcomes are unknowable in advance.
money_quotes: Because biographies of famous scientists tend to edit out their mistakes, we underestimate the degree of risk they were willing to take.
Because anything a famous scientist did that wasn't a mistake has probably now become the conventional wisdom, those choices don't seem risky either.
Maybe the smartness and the craziness were not as separate as we think.
In Newton's day the three problems seemed roughly equally promising.
Newton made three bets. One of them worked. But they were all risky.
key_insight: Discovery looks inevitable only in hindsight; at the time, even great minds are making risky bets among uncertain options.
